title: Nicomachean Ethics X: The Contemplative Life
description: Aristotle's argument that the highest human happiness is philosophical contemplation — becoming as godlike as possible.
introduction: The final book of the Nicomachean Ethics contains Aristotle's argument that the highest form of human happiness (eudaimonia) is the contemplative life — the activity of the rational soul engaged in theoretical knowledge. This is not merely an intellectual preference: Aristotle argues that contemplation makes us participate in the divine, since God's own activity is pure thought. His injunction to 'make ourselves immortal' (athanatizein) as far as possible anticipates the Christian doctrine of theosis.

translation.original_text: εἰ δὴ θεῖον ὁ νοῦς πρὸς τὸν ἄνθρωπον, καὶ ὁ κατὰ τοῦτον βίος θεῖος πρὸς τὸν ἀνθρώπινον βίον.
translation.text: If the mind is divine in comparison with the human being, then the life according to it is divine in comparison with human life.

translation.original_text: οὐ χρὴ δὲ κατὰ τοὺς παραινοῦντας ἀνθρώπινα φρονεῖν ἄνθρωπον ὄντα... ἀλλ᾿ ἐφ᾿ ὅσον ἐνδέχεται ἀθανατίζειν.
translation.text: We ought not to follow those who advise us, being human, to think human thoughts... but rather, so far as possible, to make ourselves immortal.
